docs.title: FastAPI Matrix Admin
docs.text: FastAPI Matrix Admin is the FastAPI-native admin for async SQLAlchemy teams who want an interface that feels deliberate, memorable, and operationally serious.

docs.title: Why it wins attention
docs.text:
• strong Matrix visual identity
  • pure Python integration path
  • FastAPI + SQLAlchemy focus instead of generic ORM sprawl
  • explicit permissions and row scoping
  • a simple default and a deeper escape hatch

docs.title: Why teams keep it
docs.text:
• admin.register() gives you a quick first admin
  • ModelAdmin gives you durable control for real applications
  • the request-aware model makes tenant scoping and operator workflows much easier to express

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • AI Assistants
  • Configuration
  • Comparison
  • FAQ

docs.title: Common use cases
docs.text:
• internal operations consoles
  • SaaS backoffice dashboards
  • admin tools for support teams
  • tenant-safe CRUD for SQLAlchemy applications

docs.title: Project direction
docs.text:
Matrix Admin is being optimized for the current age of AI-assisted development. That means:

  • better docs for coding assistants
  • explicit APIs that are easy to integrate correctly
  • stronger reference material for search, LLMs, and IDE assistants
  • a visual experience that makes the library instantly recognizable

docs.title: Using Matrix Admin With AI Coding Assistants
docs.text: FastAPI Matrix Admin is designed to work well in agent-driven workflows. If you use Codex, Cursor, Claude Code, Aider, or any IDE assistant, the fastest path to good results is to give the model a precise prompt and a small amount of application context.

docs.title: What to tell your assistant
docs.text:
Good prompt:

Add FastAPI Matrix Admin to this FastAPI app.
Use the existing async SQLAlchemy engine and models.
Register User, Organization, and Invoice.
Require admin login with my AdminUser model.
Scope Invoice visibility by organization for non-superusers.
Add filters for status and created_at.
Do not introduce Node.js or frontend build tooling.

This works well because it tells the assistant:

  • which framework you use
  • which ORM path to stay on
  • which models matter
  • what permissions and scoping are required
  • what not to add

docs.title: 1. Add a first admin
docs.text:
Add FastAPI Matrix Admin to this project using the existing async SQLAlchemy engine.
Auto-discover models from Base.
Mount it at /admin.
Keep the integration minimal and production-safe.

docs.title: 2. Add a serious admin
docs.text:
Integrate FastAPI Matrix Admin using ModelAdmin classes instead of only register().
Use AdminUser for authentication and AdminAuditLog for audit history.
Give Users and Organizations full CRUD to admins.
Scope invoices and tickets by the current user's organization.
Add list columns, search fields, filter fields, and CSV export.

docs.title: 3. Add a tenant-safe admin
docs.text:
Add FastAPI Matrix Admin to this multi-tenant FastAPI app.
Use row_scope so non-superusers only see records from their organization.
Protect all admin routes with the existing AdminUser auth model.
Add bulk actions only where permissions allow them.

docs.title: How to keep agent output high quality
docs.text:
• Point the assistant at the docs pages most relevant to the task.
  • Prefer ModelAdmin once your requirements include permissions or scoping.
  • Tell the assistant to reuse your existing SQLAlchemy models and auth model.
  • Tell it to keep the integration FastAPI-native and not add extra admin infrastructure.

docs.title: Docs to reference in prompts
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • Configuration
  • Integrations
  • API Reference
  • FAQ

docs.title: Recommendation for IDEs and AI agents
docs.text:
If an assistant supports project docs indexing, make sure it can see:

  • README.md
  • docs/llms.txt
  • docs/llms-full.txt
  • the docs/guide/ pages

Those files give the assistant the best picture of Matrix Admin's intended stack and integration style.

docs.title: MatrixAdmin
docs.text: Main entrypoint for integrating the admin into a FastAPI app.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    secret_key,
    *,
    engine=None,
    title="Admin",
    prefix="/admin",
    templates_dir=None,
    add_csp_middleware=True,
    max_recursion_depth=5,
    auth_model=None,
    audit_model=None,
    demo_mode=False,
    secure_cookies=None,
)

docs.title: Key arguments
docs.text:
• engine: async SQLAlchemy engine for database-backed admin features
  • auth_model: admin user model used for login and permission checks
  • audit_model: concrete audit log model to persist change history
  • secure_cookies: override secure-cookie behavior directly; otherwise use ADMIN_SECURE_COOKIES

docs.title: Key methods
docs.text:
• register(model, **options): quick registration path
  • add_view(ModelAdminSubclass): advanced registration path
  • auto_discover(Base, include=None, exclude=None): register all supported declarative models
  • get_session_dependency(): expose the admin session dependency for custom routes

docs.title: ModelConfig
docs.text:
Stored configuration for a registered model.

Notable fields:

  • list_display
  • searchable_fields
  • filter_fields
  • ordering
  • permissions
  • row_scope
  • actions
  • field_overrides
  • widgets
  • eager_load
  • detail_panels
  • menu_label
  • menu_order

docs.title: ModelAdmin
docs.text:
Declarative advanced configuration for a model. Useful when you need reusable admin behavior or more than a handful of register() options.

Example:

class UserAdmin(ModelAdmin):
    model = User
    list_display = ["id", "email", "is_active"]
    permissions = {"view": ["*"], "edit": ["admin"]}

docs.title: AdminAction
docs.text:
Defines a custom admin action.

Fields:

  • name
  • label
  • handler
  • confirmation_message
  • bulk
  • visible

docs.title: DetailPanel
docs.text: Adds custom detail content to edit/detail pages.

docs.title: DashboardCard
docs.text: Adds custom dashboard content from application-aware renderers.

docs.title: Why Teams Pick Matrix Admin
docs.text: FastAPI Matrix Admin is meant for teams who already know their stack and want the admin to fit that stack cleanly.

docs.title: If you are comparing FastAPI admin libraries
docs.text:
Choose Matrix Admin when you want:

  • FastAPI-first integration rather than a framework-agnostic abstraction layer
  • async SQLAlchemy as the main supported ORM path
  • a quick register() experience with a deeper ModelAdmin escape hatch
  • request-aware scoping for organization, tenant, or role-based data visibility
  • a UI that feels intentional and memorable instead of generic backoffice chrome

docs.title: What it is trying to be
docs.text:
The best open-source admin for FastAPI teams running real applications.

That means the project is biased toward:

  • production-safe defaults
  • explicit model registration
  • permission-aware routes
  • extension points that compose with existing FastAPI services

docs.title: What it is not trying to be
docs.text:
• a kitchen-sink multi-ORM admin
  • a separate auth platform
  • a totally neutral CRUD generator with no product opinion

That narrower scope is deliberate. It is how the library stays sharp.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
We love your input! We want to make contributing to this project as easy and transparent as possible, whether it's:

  • Reporting a bug
  • Discussing the current state of the code
  • Submitting a fix
  • Proposing new features
  • Becoming a maintainer

docs.title: We Develop with Github
docs.text: We use github to host code, to track issues and feature requests, and to accept pull requests.

docs.title: All Code Changes Happen Through Pull Requests
docs.text:
Pull requests are the best way to propose changes to the codebase (we use Github Flow). We actively welcome your pull requests:

  1. Fork the repo and create your branch from main.
  2. If you've added code that should be tested, add tests.
  3. If you've changed APIs, update the documentation.
  4. Ensure the test suite passes.
  5. Make sure your code lints.
  6. Issue that pull request!

docs.title: Any contributions you make will be under the MIT Software License
docs.text: In short, when you submit code changes, your submissions are understood to be under the same MIT License that covers the project. Feel free to contact the maintainers if that's a concern.

docs.title: Report bugs using Github's issue tracker
docs.text: We use GitHub issues to track public bugs. Report a bug by opening a new issue; it's that easy!

docs.title: Write bug reports with detail, background, and sample code
docs.text:
Great Bug Reports tend to have:

  • A quick summary and/or background
  • Steps to reproduce
    • Be specific!
    • Give sample code if you can.
  • What you expected would happen
  • What actually happened
  • Notes (possibly including why you think this might be happening, or stuff you tried that didn't work)

docs.title: License
docs.text: By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under its MIT License.

docs.title: Is this only a theme?
docs.text: No. The Matrix styling is the visual signature, but the project is moving toward a stronger FastAPI-native admin core: permissions, row scoping, bulk actions, audit hooks, and SQLAlchemy-focused ergonomics.

docs.title: Does it support SQLAlchemy?
docs.text: Yes. Async SQLAlchemy is the mainline path and the primary target for new features.

docs.title: Does it work with my existing FastAPI auth model?
docs.text: Yes. Pass your admin user model as auth_model and Matrix Admin will use it for login and permission checks.

docs.title: How do I keep data tenant-safe?
docs.text: Use row_scope() through ModelAdmin or register(..., row_scope=...) to constrain every list, export, edit, and delete query to the right slice of data.

docs.title: Do I need Node.js?
docs.text: No. The project stays intentionally Python-first.

docs.title: Is it ready for production?
docs.text:
The project is actively being hardened for production FastAPI teams. The best way to evaluate it is:

  • run the test suite
  • wire your own auth and audit models
  • use explicit model registration
  • add row scoping where your data model needs it

docs.title: Features
docs.text: FastAPI Matrix Admin is built for FastAPI developers who want a strong default and room to grow into more advanced workflows.

docs.title: 🔮 Smart Selects
docs.text:
Dealing with Foreign Keys in admin panels is usually a pain. You get a dropdown with Object <1> or have to write custom queries.

Matrix Admin changes that.

  • Auto-Detection: We automatically detect ForeignKey columns in your SQLAlchemy models.
  • Searchable Dropdowns: Instead of a plain <select>, you get a searchable, AJAX-powered widget.
  • No Config: It generally "just works". If you define __admin_repr__ on your model, we use it.
class Author(Base):
    __tablename__ = "authors"
    name = Column(String)

    def __admin_repr__(self):
        return self.name  # Use this in dropdowns

docs.title: 🧪 Advanced Filtering
docs.text:
Sidebar filters are generated automatically based on your model configuration.

admin.register(
    Product,
    filter_fields=["available", "created_at", "price"],
)
  • Boolean: Toggle switches for True/False fields.
  • Relationships: Filter by related objects (e.g., "Show products in 'Tech' category").
  • Operators: Support for __gte, __lte (Greater/Less than) suffix magic.

docs.title: 📊 System Observability
docs.text:
Your admin panel is the cockpit of your application. It should show you how the engine is running.

The Dashboard includes: - CPU Usage: Real-time load monitoring. - Memory: RAM consumption. - Disk I/O: Storage checks.

All rendered with slick, animated progress bars that fit the cyberpunk aesthetic.

docs.title: 💾 CSV Export (Streaming)
docs.text:
Need to dump data?

  • Streaming Response: We stream data row-by-row, so you can export 100,000 users without blowing up your server RAM.
  • Filter-Aware: Exports exactly what you see on the screen (current search + active filters).

docs.title: Permissions And Security
docs.text:
We take security seriously.

  • URL Signing: Polymorphic form loads use signed URLs to prevent IDOR attacks.
  • CSP Headers: Strict Content Security Policy injected automatically.
  • Rate Limiting: Login endpoints are protected against brute-force.

docs.title: MatrixAdmin
docs.text:
admin = MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    secret_key="change-me",
    engine=engine,
    title="Operations",
    prefix="/admin",
    auth_model=AdminUser,
    audit_model=AdminAuditLog,
    secure_cookies=True,
)

docs.title: Important options
docs.text:
• engine: enables database-backed CRUD, search, export, and relationship lookups
  • auth_model: enables admin login and permission checks
  • audit_model: persists create, update, and delete history
  • secure_cookies: forces secure session cookies even outside environment defaults

docs.title: register() options
docs.text:
admin.register(
    User,
    list_display=["id", "email"],
    searchable_fields=["email"],
    filter_fields=["is_active"],
    permissions={"view": ["*"], "edit": ["admin"]},
    eager_load=["organization"],
)

Common options:

  • fields
  • exclude
  • list_display
  • searchable_fields
  • filter_fields
  • ordering
  • readonly
  • permissions
  • row_scope
  • actions
  • field_overrides
  • widgets
  • eager_load
  • detail_panels
  • menu_label
  • menu_order

docs.title: ModelAdmin
docs.text:
Use ModelAdmin when you want a reusable declarative config rather than a long register() call.

class InvoiceAdmin(ModelAdmin):
    model = Invoice
    menu_label = "Invoices"
    list_display = ["id", "customer_id", "status", "created_at"]
    filter_fields = ["status", "created_at"]
    permissions = {"view": ["*"], "export": ["finance", "admin"]}

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install fastapi-matrix-admin

docs.title: Basic setup
docs.text:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine

from fastapi_matrix_admin import MatrixAdmin

app = FastAPI()
engine = create_async_engine("sqlite+aiosqlite:///./app.db")

admin = MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    engine=engine,
    secret_key="change-me-in-production",
    title="Operations",
)

docs.title: Fast path
docs.text: admin.auto_discover(Base)

docs.title: Explicit path
docs.text:
admin.register(
    User,
    list_display=["id", "email", "is_active"],
    searchable_fields=["email"],
    filter_fields=["is_active", "created_at"],
)

docs.title: Advanced path with ModelAdmin
docs.text:
from fastapi_matrix_admin import ModelAdmin


class UserAdmin(ModelAdmin):
    model = User
    menu_label = "Users"
    list_display = ["id", "email", "is_active", "created_at"]
    searchable_fields = ["email"]
    filter_fields = ["is_active", "created_at"]
    permissions = {
        "view": ["*"],
        "create": ["admin"],
        "edit": ["admin"],
        "delete": ["admin"],
        "export": ["admin"],
    }

    @staticmethod
    def row_scope(*, request, query, session, user):
        if user and not user.is_superuser:
            return query.where(User.organization_id == user.organization_id)
        return query


admin.add_view(UserAdmin)

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
If you pass auth_model=YourAdminUserModel, admin routes require authenticated users. Permissions are evaluated from each model config.

admin = MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    engine=engine,
    secret_key="change-me",
    auth_model=AdminUser,
    audit_model=AdminAuditLog,
)

Set ADMIN_SECURE_COOKIES=true in production so session cookies are marked Secure.

docs.title: Run locally
docs.text:
uvicorn main:app --reload

Open http://localhost:8000/admin.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Use your own SQLAlchemy admin user model by passing auth_model=... to MatrixAdmin.

docs.title: Audit logging
docs.text: Pass a concrete audit log model with audit_model=... to persist create, update, and delete events.

docs.title: Alembic
docs.text: If you use admin auth or audit models, include those tables in your Alembic metadata and migration flow.

docs.title: SQLModel compatibility
docs.text: SQLModel projects that expose SQLAlchemy declarative models can often use the quick registration path, but async SQLAlchemy remains the primary supported target.

docs.title: File and background workflows
docs.text: Use custom AdminAction handlers to trigger your own storage, background jobs, or internal service calls. Matrix Admin does not impose a separate job system.

docs.title: From simple register() usage to ModelAdmin
docs.text:
If your project only needs a few list and search settings, admin.register() is still the quickest option.

Move to ModelAdmin when you need:

  • model-specific permissions
  • row-level query scoping
  • reusable custom actions
  • detail panels
  • richer menu metadata

docs.title: From generic admins
docs.text:
FastAPI Matrix Admin is intentionally opinionated toward FastAPI + async SQLAlchemy. If you are migrating from a more generic admin library, expect a narrower but more FastAPI-aware integration path:

  • prefer your existing FastAPI auth model instead of a separate admin user store
  • use row_scope() for tenant-aware visibility
  • use actions for bulk workflows
  • pass an explicit audit_model if you want durable change history

docs.title: Recipe: Bulk Actions And Operator Workflows
docs.text:
Bulk actions are a good fit for real backoffice work: moderation, triage, approval, and cleanup flows.

from fastapi_matrix_admin import AdminAction, ModelAdmin


async def approve_invoices(*, request, session, model, ids, user, config):
    await session.execute(
        model.__table__.update()
        .where(model.id.in_(ids))
        .values(status="approved")
    )
    return {"ok": True, "approved": len(ids)}


class InvoiceAdmin(ModelAdmin):
    model = Invoice
    actions = [
        AdminAction(
            name="approve_invoices",
            label="Approve invoices",
            handler=approve_invoices,
        )
    ]

docs.title: When to use custom actions
docs.text:
• status transitions
  • retrying failed jobs
  • content moderation
  • support tooling
  • finance and operations approvals

Keep actions small, explicit, and audit-friendly.

docs.title: Recipe: AI-Friendly Integration
docs.text: If you want Matrix Admin to be easy for coding assistants to integrate into your app, structure the integration so the intent is obvious.

docs.title: Recommended pattern
docs.text:
1. Keep admin setup in one module.
  2. Use ModelAdmin for anything beyond a simple demo.
  3. Keep auth, audit, and row scoping explicit.
  4. Name your admin classes clearly: UserAdmin, InvoiceAdmin, TicketAdmin.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from fastapi_matrix_admin import MatrixAdmin
from .admin_views import UserAdmin, TicketAdmin

admin = MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    engine=engine,
    secret_key=settings.ADMIN_SECRET_KEY,
    auth_model=AdminUser,
    audit_model=AdminAuditLog,
)

admin.add_view(UserAdmin)
admin.add_view(TicketAdmin)

This layout gives AI assistants fewer decisions to guess at and makes maintenance easier for humans too.

docs.title: Recipe: Auth And Audit Logging
docs.text:
Use this setup when you want a proper internal admin instead of a public CRUD surface.

from fastapi_matrix_admin import MatrixAdmin

admin = MatrixAdmin(
    app,
    engine=engine,
    secret_key=settings.ADMIN_SECRET_KEY,
    auth_model=AdminUser,
    audit_model=AdminAuditLog,
    title="Operations",
)

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
• auth_model turns on authenticated admin flows
  • audit_model gives you durable create, update, and delete history
  • you keep your existing SQLAlchemy user model instead of adopting a separate auth system

docs.title: Recommended additions
docs.text:
• set ADMIN_SECURE_COOKIES=true in production
  • keep admin routes behind your existing ingress and environment controls
  • mark sensitive models readonly=True if operators should inspect but not mutate them

docs.title: Recipe: Multi-Tenant Row Scoping
docs.text:
If your app is multi-tenant, row scoping is one of the most important Matrix Admin features.

from fastapi_matrix_admin import ModelAdmin


class InvoiceAdmin(ModelAdmin):
    model = Invoice
    list_display = ["id", "customer_id", "status", "created_at"]
    searchable_fields = ["id"]
    filter_fields = ["status", "created_at"]

    @staticmethod
    def row_scope(*, request, query, session, user):
        if user is None or user.is_superuser:
            return query
        return query.where(Invoice.organization_id == user.organization_id)

docs.title: What row scoping affects
docs.text:
• list views
  • export queries
  • edit lookups
  • delete lookups
  • bulk actions

That means the same scoping rule follows the whole operator flow instead of only the first page load.
